title: Solve exponential equations using exponent properties
topic_path: Solving exponential equations using properties of exponents
Solve exponential equations using exponent properties

hints:
###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

We can rewrite $1$ as $64^{0}$, because $a^0=1$ for any non-zero real number $a$.

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$64^{7x-8}=64^{0}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 
7x-8 &=0\\\\
x &= \dfrac{8}{7}\end{align}$

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{8}{7}$ .

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{8}{7}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

We can rewrite $1$ as $26^{0}$, because $a^0=1$ for any non-zero real number $a$.

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$26^{9x+5}=26^{0}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 
9x+5 &=0\\\\
x &= -\dfrac{5}{9}\end{align}$

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-\dfrac{5}{9}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=-\dfrac{5}{9}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

We can rewrite $1$ as $56^{0}$, because $a^0=1$ for any non-zero real number $a$.

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$56^{3x-8}=56^{0}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 
3x-8&=0\\\\
x &= \dfrac{8}{3}\end{align}$

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{8}{3}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{8}{3}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

We can rewrite $1$ as $\left(\dfrac98\right)^{0}$, because $a^0=1$ for any non-zero real number $a$.

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$\left(\dfrac98\right)^{3x+12}=\left(\dfrac98\right)^{0}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 
3x+12&=0\\\\
x &= -4\end{align}$

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-4$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=-4}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

We can rewrite $1$ as $\left(\dfrac{625}{256}\right)^{0}$, because $a^0=1$ for any non-zero real number $a$.

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$\left(\dfrac{625}{256}\right)^{5x-4}=\left(\dfrac{625}{256}\right)^{0}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 
5x-4&=0\\\\
x &= \dfrac 45\end{align}$

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac 45$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x= \dfrac 45}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$2\^{4-3x}=2^{^{\scriptsize\dfrac85}}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 4-3x &=\dfrac85\\\\
x &= \dfrac{4}{5}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{4}{5}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $x=\dfrac{4}{5}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $25^{^{\scriptsize \dfrac13}}$ so its base is $5$.

$\begin{align} 25^{^{\scriptsize \dfrac13}} &= \left( 5^2 \right)^{^{\scriptsize \dfrac13}} && \text{Rewrite 25 as }5^2 \\\\
 &=5\^{2 \cdot \frac13} && \text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m}\\\\
&=5^{^{\scriptsize \dfrac23}} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$5\^{2x+7}=5^{^{\scriptsize \dfrac23}}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 2x+7 &=\dfrac23 \\\\
x &= -\dfrac{19}{6}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-\dfrac{19}{6}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $x=-\dfrac{19}{6}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $81^{^{\scriptsize -\dfrac34}}$ so its base is $3$.

$\begin{align} 81\^{-\frac34} &= \left(3^4\right)\^{-\frac34} &&\text{Rewrite 81 as }3^4 \\\\
 &=3\^{4 \cdot \left( -\frac34\right)} &&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=3^{-3} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$3\^{4x+5}=3\^{-3}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 4x+5&=-3 \\\\
x &=-2\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-2$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $x=-2$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $49^{-12}$ so its base is $7$.

$\begin{align} 49^{-12} &= (7^2)^{-12} &&\text{Rewrite 49 as }7^2 \\\\
 &=7^{2 \cdot (-12)} &&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m}\\\\
&=7^{-24} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$7^{^{\footnotesize-9x+1}}=7^{^{\footnotesize -24}}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} -9x+1 &=-24\\\\
x &= \dfrac{25}{9}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{25}{9}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $x=\dfrac{25}{9}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{27}\right)^{-8}$ so its base is $3$.

$\begin{align} \left(\dfrac{1}{27}\right)^{-8} &= \left(3^{-3}\right)^{^{\footnotesize -8}}&&\text{Rewrite } \dfrac{1}{27} \text{ as }3^{-3} \\\\
 &=3^{-3\ \cdot (-8)} &&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m}\\\\
&=3^{24} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$3^{7-2x}=3^{24}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 7-2x&=24\\\\
x &=-\dfrac{17}{2}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-\dfrac{17}{2}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $x=-\dfrac{17}{2}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{32}\right)^{3}$ so its base is $2$.

$\begin{align} \left(\dfrac{1}{32}\right)^{3} &= (2^{-5})\^{3}&&\text{Rewrite } \dfrac{1}{32} \text{ as }2^{-5} \\\\
 &=2^{-5 \cdot 3} &&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m}\\\\
&=2^{-15} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$2^{5x+8}=2^{-15}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 5x+8&=-15\\\\
x &=-\dfrac{23}{5}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-\dfrac{23}{5}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $x=-\dfrac{23}{5}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{125}\right)^{^{\scriptsize \dfrac{12}{5}}}$ so its base is $5$.

$\begin{align} \left(\dfrac{1}{125}\right)^{^{\scriptsize \dfrac{12}{5}}} &= (5^{-3})^{^{\scriptsize \dfrac{12}{5}}}&&\text{Rewrite } \dfrac{1}{125} \text{ as }5^{-3} \\\\
 &=5\^{-3 \cdot \frac{12}{5}} &&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m}\\\\
&=5\^{-\frac{36}{5}} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$5\^{x -12}=5\^{-\frac{36}{5}}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} x-12&=-\dfrac {36}{5}\\\\
x &=\dfrac{24}{5}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{24}{5}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $x=\dfrac{24}{5}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{81}\right)\^{\Large \frac 16}$ so its base is $9$.

$\begin{align} \left(\dfrac{1}{81}\right)\^{\Large \frac 16} &= (9^{-2})\^{\Large \frac 16}&&&&\text{Rewrite } \dfrac{1}{81} \text{ as }9^{-2} \\\\
 &=9\^{\Large -2\ \cdot\ \frac 16} &&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m}\\\\
&=9\^{\Large -\frac 13} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$9\^{\Large3x-10}=9\^{\Large -\frac 13}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}3x-10&=-\dfrac 13\\\\\\
x &=\dfrac{29}{9}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{29}{9}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{29}{9}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{64}\right)\^{\Large \frac {10}{3}}$ so its base is $4$.

$\begin{align} \left(\dfrac{1}{64}\right)\^{\Large \frac {10}{3}} &= (4^{-3})\^{\Large \frac {10}{3}}&&&&\text{Rewrite } \dfrac{1}{64} \text{ as }4^{-3} \\\\
 &=4\^{\Large -3\  \cdot\ \frac {10}{3}} &&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m}\\\\
&=4\^{\Large -10} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$4\^{\Large 9-6x}=4\^{\Large -10}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align} 9-6x&=-10\\\\\\
x &=\dfrac{19}{6}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{19}{6}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{19}{6}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{64}\right)\^{\Large -\frac 23}$ so its base is $8$.

$\begin{align} \left(\dfrac{1}{64}\right)\^{\Large -\frac 23} &= (8^{-2})\^{\Large- \frac 23}&&&&\text{Rewrite } \dfrac{1}{64} \text{ as }8^{-2} \\\\
 &=8\^{\Large -2\ \cdot\ -\frac 23} &&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m}\\\\
&=8\^{\Large \frac 43} \end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the equation

We obtain the following equation.

$8\^{\Large5x+3}=8\^{\Large \frac 43}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}5x+3&=\dfrac 43\\\\\\
x &=-\dfrac{1}{3}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-\dfrac{1}{3}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=-\dfrac{1}{3}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Solve the exponential equation for $x$**.

$3\^{\Large x-9}=81\^{\Large5x+1}$

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $81\^{\Large 5x+1}$ so its base is $3$.

$\begin{align}81\^{\Large5x+1}&=  (3^4)\^{\Large 5x+1} &&&&\text{Rewrite 81 as }3^4\\\\
 &=3\^{\Large 4(5x+1)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=3\^{\Large 20x+4}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$3\^{\Large x-9}=3\^{\Large 20x+4}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
x-9 &=20x+4\\\\\\
x &= -\dfrac{13}{19}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-\dfrac{13}{19}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=-\dfrac{13}{19}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Solve the exponential equation for $x$**.

$2\^{\Large 3x+5}=64\^{\Large x-7}$

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $64\^{\Large x-7}$ so its base is $2$.

$\begin{align}64\^{\Large x-7}&=  (2^6)\^{\Large x-7} &&&&\text{Rewrite 64 as }2^6\\\\
 &=2\^{\Large 6(x-7)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=2\^{\Large 6x-42}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$2\^{\Large 3x+5}=2\^{\Large 6x-42}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
3x+5&=6x-42\\\\\\
x &= \dfrac{47}{3}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{47}{3}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{47}{3}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $9\^{\Large 4x-1}$ so its base is $3$.

$\begin{align}9\^{\Large4x-1}&=  (3^2)\^{\Large 4x-1} &&&&\text{Rewrite 9 as }3^2\\\\
 &=3\^{\Large 2(4x-1)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=3\^{\Large 8x-2}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$3\^{\Large 3x-2}=3\^{\Large 8x-2}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
3x-2 &=8x-2\\\\\\
x &=0\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=0$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=0}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $125\^{\Large 2x-7}$ so its base is $5$.

$\begin{align}125\^{\Large2x-7}&=  (5^3)\^{\Large 2x-7} &&&&\text{Rewrite 125 as }5^3\\\\
 &=5\^{\Large 3(2x-7)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=5\^{\Large 6x-21}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$5\^{\Large 7x+5}=5\^{\Large 6x-21}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
7x+5 &=6x-21\\\\\\
x &= -26\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=-26$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=-26}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $16\^{\Large 5x-2}$ so its base is $2$.

$\begin{align}16\^{\Large 5x-2}&=  (2^4)\^{\Large 5x-2} &&&&\text{Rewrite 16 as }2^4\\\\
 &=2\^{\Large 4(5x-2)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=2\^{\Large 20x-8}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$2\^{\Large 9x+2}=2\^{\Large 20x-8}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
9x+2&=20x-8\\\\\\
x &= \dfrac{10}{11}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{10}{11}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{10}{11}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Solve the exponential equation for $x$**.

$3\^{\Large7x+4}=\left(\dfrac{1}{27}\right)\^{\Large x-3}$

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{27}\right)\^{\Large x-3}$ so its base is $3$.

$\begin{align}\left(\dfrac{1}{27}\right)\^{\Large x-3}&=  (3^{-3})\^{\Large x-3} &&&&\text{Rewrite }\dfrac{1}{27}\text{ as }3^{-3}\\\\
 &=3\^{\Large -3(x-3)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=3\^{\Large 9-3x}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$3\^{\Large 7x+4}=3\^{\Large 9-3x}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
7x+4&=9-3x\\\\\\
x &=\dfrac{1}{2}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{1}{2}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{1}{2}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{49}\right)\^{\Large 2x+9}$ so its base is $7$.

$\begin{align}\left(\dfrac{1}{49}\right)\^{\Large2x+9}&=  (7^{-2})\^{\Large 2x+9} &&&&\text{Rewrite }\dfrac{1}{49}\text{ as }7^{-2}\\\\
 &=7\^{\Large -2(2x+9)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=7\^{\Large -4x-18}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$7\^{\Large 3-5x}=7\^{\Large -4x-18}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
3-5x&=-4x-18\\\\\\
x &=21\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=21$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=21}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Solve the exponential equation for $x$**.

$4\^{\Large x-10}=\left(\dfrac{1}{64}\right)\^{\Large 5x+2}$

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{64}\right)\^{\Large 5x+2}$ so its base is $4$.

$\begin{align}\left(\dfrac{1}{64}\right)\^{\Large 5x+2}&=  (4^{-3})\^{\Large 5x+2} &&&&\text{Rewrite }\dfrac{1}{64}\text{ as }4^{-3}\\\\
 &=4\^{\Large -3(5x+2)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=4\^{\Large -15x-6}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$4\^{\Large x-10}=4\^{\Large -15x-6}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
x-10&=-15x-6\\\\\\
x &=\dfrac{1}{4}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{1}{4}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{1}{4}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Solve the exponential equation for $x$**.

$6\^{\Large4x-9}=\left(\dfrac{1}{36}\right)\^{\Large x-4}$

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{36}\right)\^{\Large x-4}$ so its base is $6$.

$\begin{align}\left(\dfrac{1}{36}\right)\^{\Large x-4}&=  (6^{-2})\^{\Large x-4} &&&&\text{Rewrite }\dfrac{1}{36}\text{ as }6^{-2}\\\\
 &=6\^{\Large -2(x-4)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=6\^{\Large 8-2x}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$6\^{\Large 4x-9}=6\^{\Large 8-2x}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
4x-9&=8-2x\\\\\\
x &=\dfrac{17}{6}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{17}{6}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{17}{6}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*

###The strategy

We want to rewrite one of the exponential terms in the equation so that the bases of the two terms are the same.  Then, we will be able to equate the exponents and solve for $x$. [[☃ explanation 2]]

###Matching the bases

Let's rewrite $\left(\dfrac{1}{8}\right)\^{\Large 1-5x}$ so its base is $2$.

$\begin{align}\left(\dfrac{1}{8}\right)\^{\Large 1-5x}&=  (2^{-3})\^{\Large 1-5x} &&&&\text{Rewrite }\dfrac{1}{8}\text{ as }2^{-3}\\\\
 &=2\^{\Large -3(1-5x)}&&&&\text{Since }(a^n)^m=a^{n\cdot m} \\\\
&=2\^{\Large 15x-3}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solving the linear equation

We obtained the following equation.

$2\^{\Large 6x+7}=2\^{\Large 15x-3}$

Now we can equate the exponents and solve for $x$.

$\begin{align}
6x+7&=15x-3\\\\\\
x &=\dfrac{10}{9}\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer###

The answer is $x=\dfrac{10}{9}$.

*You can check this answer by substituting $\it{x=\dfrac{10}{9}}$ in the original equation and evaluating both sides.*
